# Rupantar AI

## Agentic Operational Platform for India’s 50 Million SMB Shops

**March 2026 | Confidential**

| Core Thesis | The Platform | The Distribution Model |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| 80–85% of India's 50M shops run on paper. Not because owners don't want technology, but because no product has met them where they are: WhatsApp, voice, vernacular, and zero UI complexity. | We build an AI Agentic ERP that runs through WhatsApp and voice. Autonomous agents handle billing, inventory, compliance, customer credit, procurement, and operations. No app to install. No screen to learn. | We convert India's existing B2B field force — 1.5M FMCG reps, 6L pharma MRs, 50K Tally resellers, 3M bank DSAs — into certified 'AI Shop Doctors' who deploy and customize agents shop by shop, earning recurring income. |

# 

# Executive Brief

India's unorganised retail economy — ₹95 lakh crore across 50 million shops — operates on paper, memory, and phone calls. There is no cohesive operational layer that helps shopkeepers translate these digital rails into seamless, everyday workflows — leaving a clear need for solutions that bridge this disconnect.

The opportunity is not incremental. It is category creation: moving India's unorganised trade from paper and memory to AI-native operations delivered through WhatsApp, configurable without code, and adopted without requiring any behavioural change from the shop owner.

Rupantar can solve the problem with its Agentic Operations Platform — a composable system to build, configure, and run AI agents for real-world business workflows.

The platform contains 160+ pre-built AI agents for shop operations — billing, inventory, credit, compliance, and more. The shopkeeper interacts entirely through voice notes, photos, and one-word WhatsApp replies. We distribute through India's existing 10M+ field sales force — FMCG reps, pharma MRs, Tally resellers — converted into certified partners who use the platform to select and combine the agents into a bespoke solution for each shop, deployed through WhatsApp and voice in under 60 minutes. 

![][image1]

# 

# The Proposal

## The Opportunity

India has approximately 50 million micro and small businesses — kiranas, restaurants, pharmacies, garages, salons, fashion shops, coaching centres, and more. They collectively handle ₹95 lakh crore in annual retail and services. Yet 80–85% of them use no operational software at all. The ones that do are underserved by legacy desktop tools designed for accountants, not shopkeepers — Tally's dominance is accountant-driven, Vyapar and MyBillBook still require a UI interaction for every bill.

India’s digital public infrastructure has made significant strides — UPI enables payments, ONDC enables discovery, and ABDM manages health records. However, a critical gap remains between these systems and the day-to-day realities of running a business. There is no cohesive operational layer that helps shopkeepers translate these digital rails into seamless, everyday workflows — leaving a clear need for solutions that bridge this disconnect.

**The opportunity is not incremental. It is category creation**: moving India's unorganised trade from paper and memory to AI-native operations delivered through WhatsApp, configurable without code, and adopted without requiring any behavioural change from the shop owner.

#### The 20-Vertical Landscape

| \# | Software Area | Penetration | Existing Leaders (est. share among software users) | Gap / White Space |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| 01 | Billing & POS | \~15–20% | Tally (38%), Vyapar (13%), Marg (11%), Busy (9%), MyBillBook (7%) | 80M+ shops use paper; no voice/vernacular billing product exists |
| 02 | Inventory Mgmt | \~8–12% | Bundled with POS — Marg, GoFrugal, Tally lead. No standalone player. | Perishables, size-colour matrix, and loose-weight inventory completely unaddressed |
| 03 | Accounting & GST | \~18–22% | Tally (47%), Busy (11%), Zoho Books (7%), ClearTax (6%) | \~78% of shops file GST via CA manually; no auto-filing agent |
| 04 | Digital Payments | \~60–70% | Paytm (30%), PhonePe (30%), BharatPe (12%), Google Pay (10%) | UPI acceptance is solved; reconciliation and cash-flow intelligence are not |
| 05 | Credit Ledger / Khata | \~20–30% | Khatabook (40%), OkCredit (25%), Vyapar (20%) | 30M shops still use paper khata; no AI-driven collection intelligence |
| 06 | Restaurant / F\&B POS | \~5–8% | Petpooja (45%), Posist (10%), Rista (8%), GoFrugal (6%) | 6M+ dhabas, tea stalls, street food — zero POS penetration |
| 07 | Pharmacy Software | \~30–40% | Marg (38%), GoFrugal (13%), Pharmsoft (9%), hCue (5%) | ABDM integration, drug interaction checks, WhatsApp refill reminders — absent |
| 08 | Salon & Spa | \~3–5% | Zenoti (premium chains), Dingg (SMB), MioSalon (SMB), Invoay | \~95% of neighbourhood salons: no appointment, no staff commission, no loyalty software |
| 09 | B2B Procurement | \~5–8% | Udaan (dominant), Ninjacart (agri), Jumbotail (South India) | \~85% of retail procurement still phone-call based via distributor salesmen |
| 10 | Online Store / eComm | \~5–7% | Shopify (D2C), Dukaan (micro-SMB), Bikayi, ONDC-native apps | No zero-effort online store that auto-syncs offline inventory and handles orders |
| 11 | Fashion / Apparel | \~5–8% | Ginesys (chains), Logic ERP (SME), Unicommerce (online) | Size-colour matrix inventory for standalone garment shops — no affordable product |
| 12 | Electronics Repair | \<3% IMEI-specific | No SMB leader. Generic billing (Marg, Tally) used for billing only. | ⚠ 4M repair shops: zero job-card, IMEI tracking, or parts inventory software |
| 13 | Grocery / Kirana OS | \~8–12% | Vyapar (billing), Khatabook (ledger), Udaan (supply) | ⚠ No all-in-one kirana OS at \<₹1,000/yr with procurement \+ billing \+ credit \+ delivery |
| 14 | HR & Payroll | \~5–8% | GreytHR, Keka, Kredily — all for 10+ employee organised firms | Daily wage, shift attendance, cash payroll for retail staff — no product exists |
| 15 | Customer Loyalty / CRM | \<2% | Reelo (F\&B), WhatsApp Business (informal, free) | ⚠ No simple loyalty linked to UPI payment history without requiring customer app |
| 16 | Logistics / Delivery | \~3–5% | Shiprocket/Delhivery (ecomm). Hyperlocal delivery is entirely manual. | No WhatsApp-native last-mile delivery OS for neighbourhood shops |
| 17 | WhatsApp Commerce | \~20–30% informal; API layer \<2% | WhatsApp Business free (dominant). Interakt, AiSensy (API layer). | No vernacular, voice-note-native, full-commerce agent at \<₹500/month |
| 18 | Auto / Garage Repair | \<1% | ⚠ No clear leader. Near-zero penetration. | ⚠ 5M garages: zero digital job cards, parts inventory, or customer communication |
| 19 | Hardware / Construction Retail | \<5% | Tally (generic). No dedicated SMB hardware product. | ⚠ Rate cards per contractor, site-wise billing, material tracking — all manual |
| 20 | Education / Coaching | \~10–15% | ClassPlus, Teachmint, SimpliFee, Fedena | 7–8M solo home tutors: no fee tracking, attendance, or parent comms tool |

## Proposed Solution

Build an Agentic Operations Platform (Rupantar AI) — a composable system to build, configure, and run AI agents for real-world business workflows. Distribute it through India's existing field sales force — 1.5M FMCG reps, 600K pharma MRs, 50K Tally resellers, 3M bank DSAs, and more — trained and certified as implementation partners. They use the platform to compose solutions shop by shop, maintain shop portfolios, and earn recurring income from every active shop.

| Layer | Description | Revenue Model | Cost Structure |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Platform | Agentic Operations Platform — a composable system of 160+ AI agents deployed via WhatsApp and voice interfaces | Solution subscriptions per shop \+ platform fees \+ marketplace royalties | Platform development |
| Distribution | AI Shop Doctor partner network that configure and customize solutions shop by shop | Indirectly — enables solution sales | Sales & marketing |

### The Agentic Operations Platform

A composable system of 160+ pre-built AI agents covering every aspect of shop operations — billing, inventory, credit, compliance, and more. Each shop receives a bespoke solution composed from these agents, running on one WhatsApp number. 

* A kirana gets the following agents \- billing \+ inventory \+ credit \+ procurement \+ daily report.   
* A garage gets the following agents \- job card \+ parts inventory \+ customer update \+ vehicle history.   
* A pharmacy gets the following agents \- prescription reader \+ drug interaction check \+ expiry watch \+ refill reminder.

The shopkeeper's entire interface is voice notes, photos, and one-word WhatsApp replies. No app. No screen. No behavioural change required. 

#### Platform Architecture — Four Layers

India has 50 million shops, and every shop is different. A kirana store in Surat has 15,000 SKUs and 200 credit customers. A salon in Indore has 3 staff members and 500 client records. A garage in Pune handles 20 vehicles a day across 8 bays. No one-size-fits-all product can serve all of them.

Partners use the platform to select and combine agents for each shop — configuring triggers, setting parameters, and deploying a working solution in a single site visit. The platform's four-layer architecture makes bespoke solutions possible without engineers:

| Layer | What It Is | Primary User | Analogous To |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| L1: Agent Library | 160+ pre-built, tested, production-ready agent nodes organised by vertical and function. Each is a working agent with standard inputs/outputs, connectors, and default prompts. | Partners: deploy out-of-the-box bundles for standard shops. Shop live in \<15 minutes. | Salesforce AppExchange / Zapier templates |
| L2: Visual Flow Builder | Drag-and-drop canvas to connect agent nodes, configure triggers (WhatsApp message, UPI credit, timer, stock threshold), and set shop-specific parameters. No code required. | Partners: handle 80% of implementations. 2-day training to proficiency. | n8n canvas / Dify workflow builder — purpose-built for Indian SMB context |
| L3: Config Studio | Structured mobile wizard for shop-specific data: product catalog upload, supplier WhatsApp numbers, customer list, language preference, agent tone (formal/Hinglish/regional). Converts raw shop data to agent context. | Partners: fill in on-site during a 60-minute shop visit. Mobile-first, works on 4G. | Shopify store setup wizard / Zoho CRM onboarding |
| L4: Pro Code SDK | Python/JS SDK to build custom agent nodes, connect non-standard APIs, and publish templates to the community marketplace. Earns higher partner margins and 'Master Builder' certification. | Tier 3–4 technical partners and ex-developers building vertical specialisations. | n8n community nodes / Dify plugin system |

#### Core Platform Capabilities

| Capability | Description |
| ----- | ----- |
| WhatsApp-Native Execution | All agent actions execute through WhatsApp Business API. No separate app required by shop owner or customer. Supports text, voice notes, images, buttons, and payment links. |
| Zero-UI Design Philosophy | The shopkeeper’s entire interaction with the system is through three channels: Voice notes — spoken commands in their own language, Photos — product images, invoices, receipts for AI extraction, Short or one-word replies — "haan" to confirm an order, "nahi" to decline |
| Vernacular NLP Engine | Trained on Indian commercial vocabulary across 12 languages — understands 'adha kilo', 'khokha', 'peti', 'tola', local product aliases, and regional business idioms that global LLMs miss. |
| Offline-First Queue | All agent actions queue locally during internet outage. Sync with conflict resolution on reconnect. Critical for Tier 2–4 cities with patchy connectivity. |
| Planner → Executor → Verifier Loop | Each agent: plans the action ('send credit reminder to Ram'), executes it (sends WhatsApp), verifies outcome (checks if payment received in 48 hrs, retries with escalated tone if not). |
| Confidence Threshold | Every agent has a configurable confidence threshold below which it asks the owner for confirmation before acting. High-stakes actions (orders \>₹500, GST filing) always require explicit approval. |
| Observability (Langfuse/OpenTelemetry) | Complete audit trail of every agent decision, input, and action. Partners and Rupantar can replay any agent run. Full trace available within 48 hrs for dispute resolution. |
| Persistent Shop Memory | Each shop has an isolated memory store (customer profiles, product catalog, supplier list, transaction history, seasonal patterns). Memory compounds — agents become more accurate with every month of usage. |
| Multi-Agent Orchestration | Orchestrator agent classifies owner intent, routes to specialist domain agents, and composes responses across multiple agent outputs. Handles ambiguous or multi-step requests without breaking flow. Example: "Ram ka bill banao aur uska credit update karo, agar basmati stock kam hai toh order bhi lagao" — one voice note triggers three agents (billing, credit, inventory) simultaneously, coordinated by the orchestrator. |

#### How it Works

**AI agents handle a shopkeeper's entire daily operation through WhatsApp — the same app they already use 50 times a day.** The shopkeeper speaks and submits a voice note. The AI agent parses the intent, takes action, and sends a WhatsApp response. Zero screen interaction. Zero behavioural change required.

|  | Shopkeeper's voice note → WhatsApp → AI Agent (NLP \+ domain logic)      → Action (bill created, inventory updated, credit recorded)      → WhatsApp response (confirmation \+ any follow-up questions) |
| :---- | :---- |

This is not a chatbot. Each agent follows a **Planner → Executor → Verifier loop**. The AI Agent:

1. Plans the action ("create bill for Ram, apply credit, check stock")  
2. Executes the plan (generates bill, posts to ledger, deducts inventory), and  
3. Verifies the outcome (confirms bill sent, checks for errors). 

If confidence falls below a configurable threshold, it asks the owner before acting. High-stakes actions — orders above ₹500, GST filing (configurable) — always require explicit approval.

#### Vertical-Specific Solutions; Bespoke Implementations

Each shop does not get a generic "ERP." It gets agents configured for its vertical by the partners:

| Vertical | Solution (Agent Composition) | Key Differentiation |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Kirana Store | Billing \+ Inventory \+ Credit \+ Procurement \+ Daily Report | Voice-only operation for 20,000 SKUs, 50–200 credit customers |
| Auto Garage | Job Card \+ Parts Inventory \+ Customer Update \+ Vehicle History | Digital job cards from vehicle photos; parts compatibility tracking |
| Electronics Repair | Job Card \+ IMEI Verifier \+ Parts Inventory \+ Warranty Handler | Device identification from photos; stolen device database check |
| Pharmacy | Prescription Reader \+ Drug Interaction \+ Expiry Watch \+ Refill Reminder | Clinical safety (interaction checks); ABDM integration |
| Restaurant | Multi-Channel Order Hub \+ Kitchen Intelligence \+ Aggregator Reconciler | KOT prioritisation by prep time; daily Swiggy/Zomato payout reconciliation |
| Salon | Booking Agent \+ Client Memory \+ Empty Slot Filler \+ Staff Commission | Scales the owner's personal memory to 1,000+ clients |
| Hardware Retail | Contractor Rate Card \+ Site-wise Tracker \+ Material Calculator | Contractor-specific pricing across multiple project sites |
| Fashion | Matrix Tracker (size×colour) \+ Dead Stock Alert \+ Season Buying Advisor | Variant-level inventory for 12–40 SKUs per design |

### The Distribution Network

India doesn't have a distribution problem. It has an alignment problem. The distribution network already exists — millions of field salespeople visit shops every day. What's missing is a reason for those people to become AI implementation partners instead of just product salespeople.

#### The Existing Field Force

The distribution network already exists — millions of field salespeople visit shops every day. They know the pain points. They have the relationships. They just need a product to sell and an income model that makes it worth their time.

| Field Workforce Category | Est. Count | AI Partner Potential | Why |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| FMCG Distributor Sales Reps | \~1.5M | Very High | Already inside the shop daily, trusted, understand stock pain |
| Pharma Medical Representatives (MRs) | \~600K | Very High | Educated, visit chemists daily, manage compliance conversations |
| Fintech / POS Device Reps | \~300K+ | Very High | Device onboarding is identical to agent onboarding — natural upgrade |
| Tally / Marg / Vyapar TDPs | \~50,000+ | Very High | Already technically trained, already have SMB portfolios |
| CA Firms & Tax Consultants | \~3.5L CAs | High | Trusted financial advisor — billing \+ GST agent is a natural add-on |
| Bank / NBFC DSAs | \~3M+ | High | Already handle digital onboarding — same skill set |
| Business Correspondent Agents | \~1.2M | Medium | Unique rural reach; strong for ABDM and government-adjacent agents |
| B.Tech/MBA graduates (Tier 2–3) | Large pool | High | Motivated, trainable, deep local business community connections |

|  | Conservative target: Convert 2% of FMCG reps, pharma MRs, fintech reps, and Tally/Marg resellers \= \~100,000+ certified AI Shop Doctors. |
| :---- | :---- |

#### Partner Tier Structure

The implementation partner ecosystem is structured into defined tiers based on scale of operations and technical capability. Each tier reflects the partner’s ability to deploy, customize, and extend the Agentic Operations Platform and implement solutions.

| Tier | Name | Shop Portfolio | Technical Capability |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| T0 | Referral Agent | Refers only; no deployment | No technical skill required |
| T1 | AI Shop Starter | 5–25 active shops | Deploys standard bundles from library; no custom flows |
| T2 | AI Shop Builder | 25–150 active shops | Builds custom flows using visual builder; multi-agent configurations |
| T3 | AI Shop Architect | 150–500 active shops | Writes custom agent nodes (Python/JS); builds vertical solutions; trains T1/T2 |
| T4 | Regional Master | 500+ shops across sub-partner network | Full platform access; runs training programmes; co-develops vertical solutions with Rupantar |

#### Partner Income Model

The partner model is designed to create multiple, compounding income streams—combining recurring commissions with high-margin services and ecosystem-driven earnings as the shop base grows.

| Income Stream | Earning Mechanism | Illustrative Earnings (T2 Partner, 100 Active Shops) |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Referral Fee (only for referral agents, no deployment)** | ₹200 one-time per referred shop that activates | \- |
| **Subscription Commission (Recurring)** | 15–30% of shop's monthly subscription, earned as long as the shop remains active | 100 shops × ₹599 avg × 22% \= **₹13,178/month (recurring)** |
| **New Shop Activation Incentive** | ₹500 \- ₹2,000 per shop activated and completing the first week of active usage | 20 new shops/month × ₹1,000 \= **₹20,000/month** |
| **Customisation Services** | Partner-defined pricing for advanced/custom workflows beyond standard configurations (partner keeps 100%) | 5 implementations × ₹5,000 avg \= **₹25,000/month** |
| **Template Royalties** | ₹50–₹200 per deployment when other partners use published templates | 2 templates × 100 deployments × ₹100 \= **₹20,000/month** |
| **Annual Maintenance Contracts (AMC)** | ₹3,000–₹8,000/year per shop (partner keeps 100%) | 30 shops × ₹5,000/year \= **₹12,500/month equivalent** |
| **Total Earnings Potential** | Combined recurring \+ activation \+ services \+ ecosystem income | **\~₹90,000/month**, scaling to **₹1.5–2L/month at 200 shops** |

#### Why Partner-Led Distribution

The following benchmarks validate the choice of training and deploying partners to grow:.

| Comparable Benchmark | Key Insight | Reference Source |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Tally TDP Network**: 50K dealers generating ₹900Cr revenue | Partner-led distribution of B2B software is a proven, scalable model in India | Tally Solutions public financials |
| **Khatabook**: $275M+ raised, 30M downloads, limited monetisation | Pure self-serve SMB apps struggle with retention and monetisation without implementation support | PitchBook, TechCrunch |
| **Fintech DSA Model**: ₹15–25K/month at \~20 active clients | Field-force distribution can generate meaningful, recurring income even at modest scale | Industry benchmarks |
| **Zoho Partner Ecosystem**: 15–30% partner commissions | Revenue-sharing with partners is a well-established norm in India’s SaaS ecosystem | Zoho partner programme disclosures |
| **Petpooja**: \~₹10K/year ARPU, 1L+ outlets | SMBs in India are willing to pay for verticalised, well-implemented software solutions | Petpooja public data |

## The Long Term Vision

### The Two Horizon Framework

Rupantar AI is built with a deliberate two-horizon strategy:

* **Horizon 1:** Build a profitable, scalable operating system for small businesses  
* **Horizon 2:** Evolve into a data and services ecosystem powered by that scale

This approach ensures early revenue, controlled risk, and long-term platform upside — without requiring a pivot.

#### Horizon 1: Establish the Platform (Months 0–12)

The first phase focuses on building a high-frequency, revenue-generating system of record for shops, delivered through AI agents and a partner-led distribution model.

**What is being built**

* AI agents across key operational workflows (billing, inventory, credit, etc.)  
* A nationwide partner network (“AI Shop Doctors”) for deployment and support  
* A subscription-led business with strong unit economics

**Target outcomes**

* 50,000+ active shops with \>85% retention  
* 5,000+ active partners earning meaningful monthly income  
* \>88% voice accuracy across Hindi, Hinglish, and regional languages  
* ₹10Cr+ MRR, with profitable per-shop unit economics  
* Early evidence of multi-product stickiness (lower churn with multiple agents)

**What this validates**

* The product works in real-world conditions  
* The distribution model scales through partners  
* The unit economics are sustainable

Even at this stage, the business supports a ₹50Cr+ ARR outcome with strong margins, independent of any future ecosystem assumptions.

#### Horizon 2 \- Become the Ecosystem (Months 12–36)

As the platform scales, it naturally generates data, relationships, and network effects that unlock new revenue layers. This is not a pivot — it is value that emerges from scale.

**Stage-wise ecosystem expansion**

* At \~50,000 shops: Aggregated demand signals become valuable for brands → e.g., real-time insights on product demand across geographies  
* At \~200,000 shops: Transaction history enables embedded financial services → working capital access for shopkeepers through NBFC partnerships  
* At \~1M+ shops: A full platform ecosystem emerges → third-party developers, template marketplace, API integrations, multi-sided revenue streams

Horizon 2 does not require a separate product or a pivot. It requires the shop base, data volume, and partner network that Horizon 1 produces. It emerges from scale, not from a strategy change.

#### Downside Protection vs Upside Optionality

**Downside (if ecosystem does not materialise)**

* A profitable, subscription-led SaaS business  
* ₹50Cr+ ARR potential  
* Strong margins and predictable revenue

**Upside (if scale is achieved)**

* Data monetisation (brands, analytics)  
* Financial services (credit, underwriting)  
* Platform ecosystem (marketplace, APIs, third-party innovation)

#### Why This Approach Works

* Digitisation is the prerequisite: Shops must first become digitally active before data or financial services can exist  
* Agents are the entry point: They convert paper-based businesses into structured, usable data systems  
* Scale creates defensibility: Data, partner network, and embedded workflows become compounding advantages

# 

# Business Model, Economics & Defensibility

## Unit Economics & Growth Dynamics

### Revenue Streams

The platform is designed with a multi-layered revenue model that scales with adoption across shops, partners, and ecosystem participants. This ensures predictable SaaS income, high-margin platform revenues, and additional upside from data and financial services as the network grows.

| Revenue Stream | Paying Party | Monetisation Model | Rupantar Share (Net Realisation) |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Subscription Fees** | Shop Owners | ₹299–₹1,499/month per shop based on vertical and agent usage | \~75–85% after partner commission |
| **Agentic Operations Platform Fee** | Agent Builders / Partners | Free for Tier 1 (earn-only); ₹999/month for advanced (T2+) builders | 100% (no revenue share) |
| **Marketplace Royalties** | Shop Owners (via template usage) | Revenue split on community templates (70% creator / 30% platform) | 30% of marketplace transactions |
| **Vertical Add-on Modules** | Shop Owners | Paid premium agents with external API dependencies (e.g., ABDM, GST, aggregators) | \~60–70% margin after API costs |
| **B2B Intelligence Layer** | FMCG Brands, Banks, Enterprises | Sale of anonymised, aggregated market insights to enterprises | 100% (high-margin data revenue) |
| **Embedded Financial Services** | NBFCs / Financial Institutions | Referral/commission model on loans, insurance, BNPL via partners | \~0.5–2% commission on disbursals |

### Per-Shop Unit Economics (Stabilised, Year 2+)

The following table outlines the stabilised per-shop unit economics, highlighting strong margins, rapid payback, and highly attractive LTV/CAC dynamics driven by a partner-led distribution model. 

| Metric | Conservative | Base Case | Optimistic |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Average Monthly Subscription | ₹399 | ₹599 | ₹899 |
| Partner Commission (avg 22%) | ₹88 | ₹132 | ₹198 |
| Net Revenue to Rupantar | ₹311 | ₹467 | ₹701 |
| Cloud / LLM / API Costs per shop | ₹80 | ₹100 | ₹130 |
| Gross Profit per Shop per Month | ₹231 | ₹367 | ₹571 |
| **Gross Margin %** | **\~58%** | **\~61%** | **\~64%** |
| Customer Acquisition Cost (partner model) | ₹800 | ₹600 | ₹400 |
| Payback Period | 3.5 months | 1.6 months | 0.7 months |
| 12-Month LTV (net of commissions \+ COGS) | ₹2,772 | ₹4,404 | ₹6,852 |
| **LTV / CAC Ratio** | **3.5×** | **7.3×** | **17.1×** |

|  | A partner-led model delivers lower CAC (₹400–800 vs. ₹1,500–3,000 for direct SMB SaaS) by leveraging on-ground trust. It also improves retention—\~70% lower churn vs. self-serve (benchmark: Tally TDP-supported vs. Vyapar users) , as partners engage regularly, resolve issues early, and are financially incentivised to keep shops active.. |
| :---- | :---- |

### The Self-Reinforcing Flywheel

As the platform scales, multiple reinforcing loops begin to emerge—driving growth across partners, shops, product intelligence, and ecosystem value. This creates a compounding flywheel where each layer strengthens the others.

| Stage | What Happens | Reinforcement Effect |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **① Partner Success** | Partners deploy initial shops and begin earning meaningful recurring income (\~₹15K/month) | Demonstrated earnings attract new partners organically (3–5 inbound inquiries per active partner), reducing recruitment effort |
| **② Shop Advocacy** | Shop owners experience value and refer the solution within their network | Each active shop drives \~0.5–1 referral, progressively lowering customer acquisition cost |
| **③ Agent Intelligence Compounding** | 10,000+ active shops generate real transaction data (local SKUs, pricing, seasonality) | Improved accuracy → higher retention → higher partner earnings → stronger partner acquisition |
| **④ Community-Led Innovation** | Partners build and share reusable workflows (e.g., ABDM flows adopted across regions) | Marketplace reduces need for central engineering and enables rapid expansion into long-tail use cases |
| **⑤ Ecosystem Expansion** | External players (banks, NBFCs, FMCG brands) integrate for data, distribution, and financing | Unlocks new revenue streams beyond SaaS and strengthens overall platform defensibility |

### Value Compounding \- Revenue Progression Timeline

The revenue model evolves naturally as the shop base scales through the unlocking of value layers that only exist at scale. 

![][image2]  
Every step generates revenue. A company with 10,000 shops at ₹599/month generates ₹60 lakh MRR. At 50,000 shops, ₹3Cr MRR. The ecosystem opportunities — brand intelligence, financial services, network effects — are upside. Rupantar's revenue compounds non-linearly because the data and network effects at each level create revenue streams that don't exist in traditional SaaS.

| Timeline | Shop Base | Revenue Composition |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Year 1 (0–10K shops) | 10,000 | 100% SaaS subscriptions |
| Year 2 (10K–50K shops) | 50,000 | 85% SaaS, 10% vertical add-ons (ABDM, GST e-invoice), 5% data/analytics pilots |
| Year 3 (50K–2L shops) | 2,00,000 | 65% SaaS, 15% add-ons, 10% data/analytics, 10% financial services commissions |
| Year 4+ (2L+ shops) | 10L+ | 50% SaaS, 15% add-ons, 20% data/analytics, 15% financial services \+ ecosystem |

## Competitive Positioning

Rupantar's structural advantage is not any single feature — it is the combination of WhatsApp-native architecture, voice-first interaction, partner-led distribution, and vernacular NLP depth. No incumbent can bolt all four onto an existing product without a full rebuild.

### Incumbent Landscape

| Incumbent | Core Strength | Why They Are Structurally Unlikely to Replicate This |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Tally** (₹900Cr revenue, 50K+ TDPs) | Deepest partner ecosystem; strong accountant-led distribution and stickiness | Built for screen-first, English-first workflows. A WhatsApp/voice-native shift would disrupt their CA-driven model and legacy architecture built over 30 years |
| **Vyapar** (mobile billing/khata) | Large user base (10M+ downloads); mobile-first adoption | Still UI-driven with no voice or agent layer. Lacks partner-led distribution, leading to higher churn and limited workflow depth |
| **Petpooja** (1L+ outlets) | Strong vertical dominance in organised restaurant POS | Highly verticalised (F\&B only) with higher price points (\~₹10K/year). Does not address unorganised, long-tail businesses (e.g., dhabas, tea stalls) |
| **Zoho** (global ERP suite) | Comprehensive horizontal ERP; profitable and globally scaled | Core DNA is screen-first enterprise software. Transitioning to WhatsApp-native, vernacular, agent-led workflows requires a fundamental product rebuild, not incremental change |
| **Khatabook / OkCredit** | Massive distribution (30M+ downloads); strong funding backing | Free-first model limits monetisation. No agent layer or partner ecosystem. User base conditioned to expect zero-cost tools |
| **n8n / Dify / Langflow** | Powerful horizontal automation and agent-building platforms | Built for developers, not field operators. No native WhatsApp integration or vertical context for Indian SMB workflows; steep learning curve for non-technical users |

### Why a WhatsApp-Native Architecture Is Fundamentally Different

This is not about adding WhatsApp as a channel—it is about making it the primary operating layer. Every core action—billing, inventory checks, credit tracking, GST workflows—happens within the same WhatsApp interface the shopkeeper already uses dozens of times a day, eliminating the need for a separate app or learning curve.

For incumbents, matching this would require coordinated, high-friction shifts across multiple dimensions:

* **Interface Paradigm Shift** — Rebuilding from screen-first UI to conversational workflows (12+ months of product and UX re-architecture)  
* **Vernacular Intelligence Layer** — Training NLP systems on Indian retail language nuances across regions and dialects (multi-year data advantage)  
* **Distribution Model Transition** — Creating a partner-led onboarding, training, and support ecosystem from scratch  
* **Monetisation Realignment** — Moving from direct SaaS subscriptions to commission-driven, partner-first economics

Each of these is a standalone transformation. Executing all simultaneously requires a full-stack rethink—making replication slow, expensive, and organisationally disruptive.

## Defensibility Moats

### Multi-Layered Data Advantage

Every interaction—transactions, voice notes, and WhatsApp conversations—creates a dataset competitors cannot access. This is not just structured data, but a compounding, multi-layered context:

| Data Layer | What It Captures | Strategic Value |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Transaction** | Products sold, pricing, customer-level demand | Real-time demand signals, pricing intelligence, seasonality patterns |
| **Behavioural** | Credit habits, reorder cycles, response patterns | Predictive models for churn, upsell, and financial services |
| **Conversational** | Vernacular language, product aliases, local phrasing | Proprietary NLP training data unavailable to global models |
| **Relationship** | Supplier networks, customer graphs, credit dependencies | Network map of India’s unorganised retail economy |
| **Temporal** | Festival cycles, local events, weekly rhythms | Forecasting for inventory and procurement decisions |

Others see events. This sees intent, behaviour, and relationships together. This is not just a dataset—it is a compounding intelligence layer that gets harder to displace every month.

### Behavioural Lock-In

As daily operations—customers, suppliers, transactions, and credit—converge into a single WhatsApp identity, switching becomes structurally difficult. This is not software lock-in, but workflow lock-in.

Once billing, credit, suppliers, and customers converge into a single WhatsApp identity:

* There is no clean data export  
* There is no parallel system  
* There is no downtime tolerance

Switching means rebuilding the business’s operating memory from scratch. That rarely happens.

### Vernacular Intelligence Moat

The system continuously learns regional languages, dialects, and business-specific vocabulary—down to local units, product names, and contextual meanings.

A “tola” is not just a unit—it is context-dependent. This level of granularity is absent in global models and horizontal platforms, which lack both the data and the incentive to optimize for Indian retail vernacular.

This is not just language support—it is commercial understanding embedded in language. Each interaction deepens this advantage, making it increasingly difficult to replicate.

### Partner Network Moat

The distribution layer is not just a channel—it is an income network. Partners build recurring revenue streams tied to their shop base, aligning long-term incentives with platform success. Their livelihood is connected with the platform. This creates economic lock-in, not just distribution scale.

To compete, a new entrant must:

* Match partner earnings  
* Rebuild trust at scale  
* Migrate live shop operations

This is a multi-year, execution-heavy problem with high failure risk—as proven by the durability of Tally’s ecosystem.

### Community-Driven Expansion

Long-tail use cases are solved by the ecosystem, not the core team. 

* Every partner builds for their niche  
* Every template becomes reusable  
* Every deployment expands coverage

This creates a living library of micro-products across geographies and verticals that a new entrant would take years to replicate from scratch.

# The Execution

## Implementation Plan

The execution follows a phased rollout: validate in one city, prove unit economics, expand geography, deepen verticals, and build the platform — each phase independently valuable and each producing an exit ramp for the chairman.

### Build Sequence

| Phase | Timeline | Product & Platform Build | Distribution & Partner Scale | Key Milestones & Metrics |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Phase 0: Foundation** | M0–3 | Core WhatsApp-native agent infrastructure (orchestrator \+ domain agent framework). Launch Billing, Khata, and Daily Reporting agents. Config Studio (partner onboarding app). Offline sync layer. | Seed 50–100 partners from Tally/Marg ecosystems in Ahmedabad/Surat. Launch Tier 1 training cohort. | 500–1,000 active shops. \>85% voice billing accuracy. Initial partner satisfaction benchmarks established |
| **Phase 1: Revenue Activation** | M3–6 | Launch vertical agents: Restaurant POS, Garage OS, Electronics Repair. Introduce Visual Flow Builder (Layer 2\) and Portfolio Health Dashboard. | Expand to Pune, Nashik, Bengaluru. Scale to 500–1,000 T1 partners. Launch first Tier 2 cohort. | 10,000 shops. ₹1Cr MRR. 80%+ partners earning \>₹15K/month |
| **Phase 2: Vertical Expansion** | M6–12 | Deepen vertical stack: Pharmacy (ABDM), Loyalty CRM, Salon, Fashion Inventory. Launch Template Marketplace (alpha) and Pro Code SDK (T3). | Expand across Hindi belt (Delhi NCR, Jaipur, Lucknow, Indore). Scale to 5,000+ partners. Pilot Regional Masters. | 50,000 shops. ₹10Cr MRR. Cross-vertical retention established. 50+ community templates live |
| **Phase 3: Full Stack OS** | M12–18 | Expand into full business OS: HR/Payroll, Accounting \+ GST auto-filing, B2B procurement, hardware retail, online storefronts. Complete 20-vertical coverage. | National expansion (Chennai, Hyderabad, Kolkata \+ Tier 3 via BC agents). 10,000+ partners. Regional Masters operational. | 2,00,000 shops. ₹50Cr ARR trajectory. Platform becomes primary system of record |
| **Phase 4: Platform Economy** | M18+ | Launch data intelligence layer, embedded financial services, ONDC integrations. Transition toward voice-first interface (beyond WhatsApp). | 50,000+ certified partners. Self-sustaining Regional Master ecosystem. 100+ templates/month from community. | 10L+ shops. Multi-stream revenue. Data monetisation active. IPO-scale metrics |

Each platform build milestone directly expands the ecosystem — the agent library, the data layer, the partner network, and the revenue streams all compound with every phase.

### Geographic Rollout Strategy

| Phase | Target Markets | Strategic Rationale | Primary Partner Archetype |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Seed (M0–3)** | Ahmedabad, Surat, Baroda (Gujarat) | High density of business-oriented SMBs with strong willingness to adopt paid software. Proven market (e.g., Petpooja). Dense Tally TDP ecosystem and existing network advantage. | Tally/Marg TDPs, fintech POS sales reps, CA firms |
| **Phase 1 (M3–6)** | Pune, Nashik (Maharashtra) \+ Bengaluru | Maharashtra: large SMB base (8M+) with strong pharma distribution networks. Bengaluru: early adopters with higher willingness to pay and tech familiarity. | Pharma MRs (Maharashtra), tech-savvy operators and ex-startup talent (Bengaluru) |
| **Phase 2 (M6–12)** | Delhi NCR, Jaipur, Lucknow, Indore, Bhopal | High concentration of undigitised kirana stores and dense FMCG distribution networks. Large opportunity for first-time software adoption at scale. | FMCG distributor sales reps, Tier 2 engineering graduates |
| **Phase 3 (M12+)** | Chennai, Hyderabad, Kolkata \+ Tier 3–4 expansion via BC agents | South: strong pharma and operational discipline (Chennai), tech adoption (Hyderabad). Kolkata: trading and fashion hub. Tier 3–4 unlocked via assisted distribution. | Pharma MRs (South), banking correspondents (rural), local engineering graduates |

## Prioritizing Among the Verticals

### The Prioritisation Framework

All opportunities are evaluated across six dimensions (scored 1–5). Final score \= weighted average.

| Dimension | Weight | Evaluation Criteria |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Market Size** | 20% | Total addressable shop base within the vertical |
| **AI Leverage** | 20% | Degree of improvement AI delivers over traditional software workflows |
| **Technical Feasibility** | 20% | Ease of building with current LLM and agent capabilities |
| **White Space** | 20% | Absence of dominant incumbents or entrenched solutions |
| **Monetisation Potential** | 10% | Clear willingness to pay and pricing power |
| **Defensibility Potential** | 10% | Ability to build long-term moat and resist replication |

### Tiering & Execution Strategy

| Tier | Score Range | Execution Approach |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **T1 ★ (Priority)** | ≥87% | Build immediately. First-mover advantage is critical. Aggressive partner-led rollout to define the category |
| **T1** | 77–86% | Build in Phase 1\. Target existing software users and position as a clear AI-driven upgrade |
| **T2** | 63–76% | Build in Phase 2\. Prioritise based on demand signals from active shop base |
| **T3** | \<63% | Do not build. Integrate or partner with existing solutions where required |

### Ranked Opportunity Map

| Rank | Vertical / Use Case | Market Size | AI Advantage | Technical Feasibility | White Space | Monetisation | Defensibility | Score | Tier |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| 1 | Kirana Store OS | 5 | 5 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 4 | **90%** | **T1 ★** |
| 2 | Auto Garage OS | 4 | 5 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 5 | **90%** | **T1 ★** |
| 3 | Electronics Repair | 4 | 5 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 5 | **90%** | **T1 ★** |
| 4 | Customer Loyalty CRM | 5 | 5 | 4 | 5 | 3 | 4 | **87%** | **T1 ★** |
| 5 | WhatsApp Commerce | 5 | 5 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 3 | **83%** | **T1 ★** |
| 6 | Restaurant F\&B POS | 4 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 3 | **80%** | **T1** |
| 7 | Hardware Retail OS | 3 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 5 | **80%** | **T1** |
| 8 | Billing & POS | 5 | 5 | 4 | 2 | 5 | 2 | **77%** | **T1** |
| 9 | Salon & Spa OS | 3 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 4 | **77%** | **T1** |
| 10 | Accounting & GST | 5 | 4 | 4 | 2 | 5 | 2 | **73%** | **T2** |
| 11 | Fashion Apparel | 4 | 4 | 3 | 4 | 4 | 3 | **73%** | **T2** |
| 12 | Online Store Builder | 4 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | **70%** | **T2** |
| 13 | Education / Coaching | 3 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 3 | **70%** | **T2** |
| 14 | Inventory Management | 4 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | **70%** | **T2** |
| 15 | Pharmacy Software | 4 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 5 | 2 | **67%** | **T2** |
| 16 | Credit Ledger | 4 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 2 | **67%** | **T2** |
| 17 | B2B Procurement | 4 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 4 | 2 | **67%** | **T2** |
| 18 | HR & Payroll | 3 | 3 | 4 | 3 | 4 | 3 | **67%** | **T2** |
| 19 | Logistics & Delivery | 4 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 2 | **63%** | **T2** |
| 20 | Digital Payments | 5 | 3 | 3 | 1 | 3 | 1 | **53%** | **T3** |

## Platform Architecture

| Decision Area | Selected Approach | Rationale |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Cloud Infrastructure** | Multi-region Indian cloud deployment (AWS Mumbai / GCP Mumbai) | Ensures data localisation compliance and provides low-latency access for Indian shop operations |
| **WhatsApp Integration** | Official WhatsApp Business API integrated via Meta Graph API | Maintains compliance with Meta policies, prevents account bans, and enables native features such as WhatsApp Pay |
| **LLM Provider Strategy** | Multi-provider abstraction layer (Gemini, GPT-4, Claude) | Avoids vendor lock-in, enables task-based model routing for cost/performance optimisation, and provides failover resilience |
| **Primary Data Layer** | PostgreSQL (primary DB) with Redis (hot cache) | Ensures strong transactional integrity while supporting low-latency access for sessions and real-time processing |
| **Agent State Management** | Event-sourced architecture with persistent shop memory | Enables full auditability, replay capability, and traceability of all agent actions as immutable events |
| **Observability & Monitoring** | Langfuse integrated with OpenTelemetry | Provides end-to-end visibility into agent decisions, cost tracking per shop, and system performance monitoring |

## Key Risks and Mitigation Strategy

| Risk Area | Risk Summary | Mitigation Approach |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Credibility Gap** | Early deployment scale (single city) may not reflect long-term ecosystem vision | Use a defined **Ecosystem Readiness Scorecard**; track and communicate both unit economics and ecosystem milestones transparently |
| **Platform Dependency (Meta)** | Reliance on WhatsApp Business API for core product experience | Maintain **channel-agnostic architecture** (voice, SMS fallback); build moat in **data, partner network, and NLP**, not interface layer |
| **Voice Accuracy** | Real-world conditions may reduce automation accuracy | Validate in controlled pilots; fallback to **human-in-the-loop workflows** to preserve usability if full automation is not viable |
| **Partner Attrition** | Low partner income or complexity may lead to drop-offs | Enforce **performance-based certification**, validate early partner income, and provide **portfolio monitoring tools** |
| **Scope Creep** | External stakeholders may push premature integrations | Apply **“One Customer Rule”** (shopkeeper-first) and a **gated partnership framework** for controlled expansion |
| **Data Compliance (DPDP)** | Regulatory risks from handling transactional and personal data | Implement **privacy-by-design**, consent management, anonymisation, and India-localised data storage |
| **Competition** | Entry by large players with capital and distribution | Focus on **speed \+ partner network scale**; create switching friction; maintain **strategic acquisition optionality** |
| **Regulatory Changes** | Evolving AI, fintech, and data regulations | Engage proactively with regulators; ensure **auditability and explainability** are built into the platform |
| **Unit Economics** | Potential margin pressure from LLM costs, ARPU, or commissions | Use **multi-provider LLM routing**, conservative cost assumptions, low-CAC partner model, and **ARPU expansion levers** |
| **Technology Dependency** | Dependence on cloud, LLMs, and APIs | Design **provider-agnostic architecture** (cloud \+ LLM); diversify channels; anchor value in **data and network** |

# Appendices

## Appendix A: Market Landscape 20 Software Areas

Each vertical below represents a credible expansion pathway for the platform. The objective is not to pursue all categories at once, but to show that the core operating model — voice-first, WhatsApp-native, AI-led workflows for small businesses — can be extended across multiple high-fragmentation retail and service sectors.

### Area 01 — Billing, POS & Invoicing

| Addressable Market | \~50M shops |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 15–20% |
| **Current Leaders** | Tally (38%), Vyapar (13%), Marg (11%), Busy (9%), MyBillBook (7%) |

**Why this market matters**  
Billing is the daily operating core of every shop, yet most products are built for accountants or computer-literate operators. A large majority of Indian shops still rely on paper because software requires screens, menus, and manual entry.

**What we build**  
A **voice-first billing agent** that converts spoken orders into GST-compliant invoices, applies customer-specific pricing or credit, and sends the bill over WhatsApp — with no screen interaction required.

**Key capabilities**

* Voice-to-bill in Hindi, Hinglish, and regional languages  
* Product scan via barcode or image for instant SKU recognition  
* Customer memory for pricing, credit, and purchase history  
* Automated GST logic, validation, and invoice generation  
* End-of-day reconciliation across cash, UPI, and credit  
* Offline transaction capture with sync on reconnect

### Area 02 — Inventory Management

| Addressable Market | \~50M shops; 10–15M with meaningful inventory complexity |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 8–12% |
| **Current Leaders** | Marg ERP, GoFrugal, Tally (typically bundled) |

**Why this market matters**  
Most shops do not actively manage inventory; they discover stockouts only when a customer asks for an item. This leads to lost sales, overstocking, and cash inefficiency.

**What we build**  
An **autonomous stock intelligence agent** that tracks inventory in real time, predicts stockouts before they happen, and prepares purchase orders for one-click approval on WhatsApp.

**Key capabilities**

* Real-time stock deduction from every sale  
* Demand forecasting using sales velocity and seasonality  
* Automated reorder recommendations and supplier routing  
* Expiry tracking for grocery, pharma, and FMCG  
* Shrinkage and variance detection  
* New product onboarding from invoice or image capture

### Area 03 — Accounting & GST Compliance

| Addressable Market | \~10M GST-registered shops |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 18–22% |
| **Current Leaders** | Tally (47%), Busy (11%), Zoho Books (7%), ClearTax (6%), Vyapar (5%) |

**Why this market matters**  
Compliance remains one of the most painful and outsourced workflows for small businesses. Many shopkeepers depend on accountants for routine GST and bookkeeping tasks that can be automated.

**What we build**  
An **autonomous compliance agent** that posts transactions to the right ledger, prepares GST returns, reconciles inputs, and enables filing with a single owner approval.

**Key capabilities**

* Auto-bookkeeping from billing, payment, and expense flows  
* GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B preparation and filing support  
* e-Way bill generation  
* Receipt and expense extraction from photos  
* WhatsApp-based monthly P\&L summaries in plain Hindi  
* Filing deadline tracking and exception alerts

### Area 04 — Digital Payments & UPI Intelligence

| Addressable Market | \~50M shops accepting digital payments |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 60–70% for payment acceptance; low for reconciliation intelligence |
| **Current Leaders** | Paytm, PhonePe, BharatPe, Google Pay, Pine Labs |

**Why this market matters**  
Payment acceptance is already widespread. The unsolved layer is not collection — it is reconciliation, dues follow-up, and cash-flow visibility.

**What we build**  
A **payment intelligence layer** that sits on top of existing UPI rails, auto-matches payments, follows up on receivables, and forecasts short-term cash position.

**Key capabilities**

* Automated UPI reconciliation against open orders and ledgers  
* Smart follow-up for unpaid balances  
* 14- and 30-day cash-flow forecasting  
* Fraud detection for fake payment confirmations  
* Daily register settlement across cash and digital modes

### Area 05 — Credit Ledger (Khata / Udhar)

| Addressable Market | \~25–30M shops that extend credit |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 20–30% |
| **Current Leaders** | Khatabook, OkCredit, Vyapar |

**Why this market matters**  
Digital khata products achieved scale but struggled to monetise because they digitised record-keeping, not collections. The economic value lies in improving repayment.

**What we build**  
An **intelligent credit agent** that manages the full credit lifecycle — from voice-based entry to reminder timing to credit scoring for future lending access.

**Key capabilities**

* Voice-based credit entry  
* Collection reminders timed to customer payment behaviour  
* Risk scoring and credit limit alerts  
* Early warning signals for bad debt  
* Creditworthiness summaries for lending partners

### Area 06 — Restaurant & F\&B POS

| Addressable Market | \~7.5M food service establishments |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 5–8% |
| **Current Leaders** | Petpooja, Posist/Restroworks, Rista/DotPe |

**Why this market matters**  
Restaurant POS is already a category, but smaller food businesses still lack operational intelligence around kitchen flow, aggregator reconciliation, and daily profitability.

**What we build**  
A **restaurant co-manager agent** that coordinates orders across channels, optimises kitchen execution, reconciles marketplace payouts, and briefs the owner daily.

**Key capabilities**

* Unified order handling across counter, aggregator, WhatsApp, and QR  
* Kitchen queue optimisation by prep time  
* Swiggy/Zomato payout reconciliation and dispute support  
* Menu profitability insights  
* Inventory-linked item availability  
* Daily owner summary with actions for the next shift

### Area 07 — Pharmacy & Chemist Software

| Addressable Market | \~1.2M registered chemists \+ \~300K unregistered |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 30–40% |
| **Current Leaders** | Marg ERP, GoFrugal, Pharmsoft, hCue, eVitalRx |

**Why this market matters**  
Pharmacy already has higher software penetration due to regulation, but legacy systems remain workflow-heavy and offer little intelligence beyond record-keeping.

**What we build**  
A **pharmacy intelligence agent** that automates billing and compliance while adding patient safety, refill management, and ABDM readiness.

**Key capabilities**

* Prescription reading from image capture  
* Drug interaction and dispensing checks  
* Expiry and return management  
* Refill reminders for chronic patients  
* Automated distributor ordering  
* ABDM integration readiness

### Area 08 — Salon, Spa & Beauty

| Addressable Market | \~2M salons and beauty parlours |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 3–5% |
| **Current Leaders** | Zenoti, Dingg, MioSalon, Invoay |

**Why this market matters**  
Most neighbourhood salons run on owner memory, paper booking, and WhatsApp. The key challenge is not software adoption — it is preserving personalised customer experience at scale.

**What we build**  
A **client memory and revenue agent** that manages bookings, service history, preferences, repeat visits, and staff commissions.

**Key capabilities**

* Omnichannel appointment booking  
* Rich customer memory (service history, formulas, preferences)  
* Gap-filling outreach to reduce idle time  
* Staff incentive tracking  
* Automated review generation and retention nudges

### Area 09 — B2B Procurement / Supply Chain

| Addressable Market | \~15M+ shops restocking from distributors |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 5–8% |
| **Current Leaders** | Udaan, Ninjacart, Jumbotail |

**Why this market matters**  
B2B commerce players have digitised ordering, but not the in-store intelligence that determines what to order, when, and from whom.

**What we build**  
A **smart procurement agent** that monitors stock, compares supplier economics, places replenishment orders, and validates delivery against order terms.

**Key capabilities**

* Automated purchase order generation  
* Supplier selection based on pricing and scheme economics  
* Delivery verification and shortage detection  
* Invoice matching and discrepancy alerts  
* Credit tracking across suppliers

### Area 10 — Online Store / eCommerce Enablement

| Addressable Market | \~5–7M shops attempting online sales |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 5–7% active online stores |
| **Current Leaders** | Shopify, Dukaan, Bikayi, ONDC-native apps |

**Why this market matters**  
Many small businesses “go online” but fail to maintain catalogues, process orders consistently, or respond to customer queries in time.

**What we build**  
An **online store manager agent** that keeps the online channel updated, answers customer queries, processes orders, and runs simple campaigns with minimal owner involvement.

**Key capabilities**

* Real-time catalogue sync from offline inventory  
* Automated customer support across chat channels  
* Order confirmation, courier booking, and tracking  
* Promotion creation based on inventory and local demand  
* ONDC seller-side workflow management

### Area 11 — Fashion, Garments & Apparel Retail

| Addressable Market | \~4–5M garment shops |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 5–8% |
| **Current Leaders** | Ginesys, Logic ERP, Unicommerce |

**Why this market matters**  
Fashion retail has unique operational complexity due to style-size-colour variants and high dead-stock risk. This remains underserved for small independent retailers.

**What we build**  
A **fashion intelligence agent** that manages variant-level inventory, identifies dead stock early, and supports better markdown and buying decisions.

**Key capabilities**

* Variant matrix tracking  
* Dead-stock and ageing alerts  
* Season-end markdown guidance  
* Buying recommendations based on sell-through  
* Bundle optimisation and supplier quality tracking

### Area 12 — Electronics Repair & Mobile Shops

| Addressable Market | \~4–5M mobile and electronics repair shops |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | \<3% |
| **Current Leaders** | No clear SMB leader |

**Why this market matters**  
This is a fragmented, mostly offline category with no meaningful software incumbent. Shops still rely on handwritten job cards and informal customer communication.

**What we build**  
A **mobile repair operating system** that creates digital job cards, tracks repair status, manages parts, and updates customers automatically through WhatsApp.

**Key capabilities**

* Job card creation from photos and issue descriptions  
* IMEI verification and history tracking  
* Spare parts inventory and ordering  
* Automated customer status updates  
* Repair estimate support and warranty workflow assistance

### Area 13 — Grocery / Kirana Store OS

| Addressable Market | \~12M kirana stores |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 8–12% |
| **Current Leaders** | Vyapar, Khatabook, Udaan (point solutions only) |

**Why this market matters**  
Kirana is the largest and most strategic SMB category in India, but also the hardest to serve. Owners are time-constrained, often non-digital, and cannot adopt software that requires behavioural change.

**What we build**  
A **zero-UI kirana operating system** that runs through a single WhatsApp number. Billing, credit, stock, ordering, delivery coordination, and daily reporting are all voice-driven.

**Key capabilities**

* Voice-first command centre for daily operations  
* Home delivery coordination  
* Customer memory and repeat order intelligence  
* Festival demand planning  
* Working capital readiness through transaction-based scoring  
* Daily business summary in Hindi voice format

### Area 14 — HR & Payroll

| Addressable Market | \~3–5M shops with 5+ staff |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 5–8% |
| **Current Leaders** | GreytHR, Keka, Kredily, Razorpay Payroll |

**Why this market matters**  
For small businesses with staff, payroll and labour compliance are recurring pain points that remain highly manual.

**What we build**  
An **invisible HR agent** that handles attendance, payroll, salary disbursement, and compliance in the background, surfacing only approvals or exceptions.

**Key capabilities**

* Attendance capture via WhatsApp, QR, or face scan  
* Payroll automation including deductions and advances  
* Salary transfer coordination  
* PF/ESI filing support  
* Labour compliance tracking and alerts

### Area 15 — Customer Loyalty & CRM

| Addressable Market | \~50M shops with repeat customers |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | \<2% |
| **Current Leaders** | Reelo, informal WhatsApp broadcasts, Capillary (enterprise) |

**Why this market matters**  
Most small businesses already practice informal CRM through personal relationships. The challenge is scaling that memory beyond a few dozen regular customers.

**What we build**  
A **hyper-personalisation agent** that captures customer history, identifies the right outreach moment, and increases repeat visits through relevant communication.

**Key capabilities**

* Customer 360 profiles  
* Personalised campaign timing  
* Occasion-based offers  
* Win-back workflows  
* Referral campaigns  
* Campaign performance learning loop

### Area 16 — Logistics & Last-Mile Delivery

| Addressable Market | \~5–8M shops doing home delivery |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 3–5% |
| **Current Leaders** | Limited SMB-focused incumbency; organised players focus on e-commerce |

**Why this market matters**  
Neighbourhood delivery remains highly manual, with poor visibility for both shopkeeper and customer.

**What we build**  
A **hyperlocal delivery agent** that assigns orders, provides WhatsApp-based tracking, optimises routes, and handles failed deliveries automatically.

**Key capabilities**

* Smart rider assignment  
* WhatsApp-native customer tracking  
* Multi-drop route optimisation  
* Failed delivery recovery workflows  
* COD collection tracking

### Area 17 — WhatsApp Commerce

| Addressable Market | \~50M shops |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 20–30% informal usage; \<2% structured API-led commerce |
| **Current Leaders** | WhatsApp Business, Interakt, AiSensy, Wati |

**Why this market matters**  
WhatsApp is already the de facto communication and commerce layer for Indian SMBs, but today it is still largely manual and fragmented.

**What we build**  
A **full-stack WhatsApp commerce agent** that handles product discovery, order taking, payment, support, and post-purchase communication in the customer’s own language.

**Key capabilities**

* Conversational catalogue  
* Voice order capture  
* Multilingual customer interaction  
* Automated FAQs and support  
* Payment link issuance and confirmation  
* Campaign management and targeting

### Area 18 — Auto Repair & Garage Management

| Addressable Market | \~5M garages and workshops |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | \<1% |
| **Current Leaders** | No scaled incumbent |

**Why this market matters**  
Garage management in India remains almost entirely manual. Workflow digitisation is extremely limited despite recurring customer, parts, and service complexity.

**What we build**  
A **garage operating system** that manages job cards, diagnostics, parts ordering, repair updates, and vehicle history through WhatsApp-based workflows.

**Key capabilities**

* Digital job card creation  
* Fault diagnosis assistance  
* Spare parts ordering workflows  
* Automated customer status updates  
* Vehicle service history  
* Workshop capacity management

### Area 19 — Hardware & Construction Retail

| Addressable Market | \~2–3M hardware and building material shops |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | \<5% |
| **Current Leaders** | Mostly generic accounting software |

**Why this market matters**  
This category has complex commercial logic — contractor pricing, site-level billing, delivery challans, and extended credit — that generic SME software handles poorly.

**What we build**  
A **contractor relationship agent** that manages custom pricing, site-wise material tracking, credit exposure, and purchase bill digitisation.

**Key capabilities**

* Dynamic contractor-specific pricing  
* Site-level delivery and billing records  
* Credit monitoring by contractor and project  
* Supplier bill digitisation  
* Material estimation and quote generation

### Area 20 — Education & Coaching Centres

| Addressable Market | \~3M coaching centres \+ 7–8M solo tutors |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Software Penetration** | 10–15% for organised centres; \<1% for solo tutors |
| **Current Leaders** | ClassPlus, Teachmint, SimpliFee, Fedena |

**Why this market matters**  
The organised coaching segment is increasingly served, but solo tutors and micro-centres still run fee collection, attendance, and parent communication manually.

**What we build**  
A **tutor operating system** that manages fees, attendance, parent updates, and simple performance reporting over WhatsApp.

**Key capabilities**

* Fee reminders and receipts  
* Attendance tracking  
* Parent communication workflows  
* Progress report generation  
* Lead qualification and enrollment follow-up

## 

## Appendix B: Partner Programme Details

### B1. Partner Tier Ladder

The partner ecosystem is designed as a **progressive capability and income ladder**, enabling scale without linear hiring. Partners evolve from lead generation to full-stack implementation and regional ownership.

| Tier | Role | Scope of Responsibility | Portfolio Scale | Capability Level |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **T0** | Referral Agent | Lead generation only | No owned portfolio | Non-technical |
| **T1** | AI Shop Starter | Deploys standard solutions | 5–25 shops | Basic deployment |
| **T2** | AI Shop Builder | Configures custom workflows | 25–150 shops | No-code / low-code |
| **T3** | AI Shop Architect | Builds advanced solutions and trains partners | 150–500 shops | Code \+ vertical expertise |
| **T4** | Regional Master | Manages partner networks and regional scale | 500+ shops (network) | Strategic \+ ecosystem |

### B2. Partner Income Streams

The income model is designed to reward both **customer acquisition** and **portfolio quality over time**.  
As partners move up the ladder, their revenue shifts from one-time activation fees toward higher-margin recurring income, services revenue, and ecosystem participation.

| Revenue Stream | How It Works | T0 | T1 | T2 | T3 | T4 |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Referral Amount** | Amount earned for each referral that converts | ₹200 | — | — | — | — |
| **Subscription Commission** | Share of monthly subscription revenue from managed shops | — | 15% | 22% | 30% | 35% |
| **Activation Fee** | One-time payment when a shop goes live and remains active | — | ₹500 | ₹1,000 | ₹2,000 | ₹2,000 |
| **Customisation Revenue** | Fees charged for non-standard workflows and deployment work | — | — | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Template Royalties** | Earnings from marketplace templates deployed by other partners | — | — | — | Yes | Yes |
| **AMC / Support Contracts** | Annual maintenance and support fees retained by partner | — | — | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Sub-Partner Override** | Revenue share on downstream partner network performance | — | — | — | — | Yes |

### B3. Partner Training Path

The training system is designed as a **capability pipeline**, not a one-time onboarding.

| Stage | Focus | Outcome |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Foundation** | Product understanding and shop pain mapping | Partners can sell and position effectively |
| **Deployment** | Standard solution rollout | Partners can activate and onboard shops independently |
| **Builder** | Workflow configuration (visual tools) | Partners can customise solutions |
| **Specialisation** | Vertical expertise (pharmacy, restaurant, etc.) | Higher-value deployments and upsell |
| **Advanced (Code)** | SDK and integrations | Creation of reusable components and templates |
| **Trainer Layer** | Train-the-trainer programmes | Regional scaling through sub-partners |

### B4. Training Curriculum

| Module | Duration | Format | Purpose | Required For |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **M0: AI Basics for Shop Operations** | 3 hours | Mobile video \+ quiz | Introduces AI agents, WhatsApp-native workflows, and how to explain the product simply to shopkeepers | All tiers |
| **M1: Shop Pain Discovery** | 4 hours | Guided role-play | Trains partners to identify operational pain points, match them to the right solution, and handle objections | T1+ |
| **M2: Standard Bundle Deployment** | 6 hours | Hands-on lab | Covers deployment of core starter solutions and configuration of language, catalog, and workflow settings | T1+ |
| **M3: Visual Flow Builder** | 2 days | Workshop \+ lab | Teaches partners to build and test customised workflows using the no-code / low-code builder | T2+ |
| **M4: Vertical Specialisation** | 1 day per vertical | Deep-dive workshop | Provides category-specific operational expertise across priority verticals such as pharmacy, restaurant, fashion, garage, and hardware | T2+ |
| **M5: Portfolio Management** | 4 hours | Dashboard \+ mentorship | Focuses on account health, upsell opportunities, issue resolution, and referral-led portfolio growth | T2+ |
| **M6: Pro Code SDK** | 3 days | Developer workshop | Enables advanced partners to write custom nodes, integrate APIs, and contribute reusable templates | T3+ |
| **M7: Train the Trainer** | 2 days | Instructor workshop | Prepares regional leaders to certify new partners, maintain quality, and run regional expansion programmes | T4 only |

### B5. Certification Framework

Certification is based on **demonstrated field performance**, not classroom completion alone.  
This is important because it ensures that tier progression reflects real operating capability and measurable partner contribution.

| Tier | Qualification Standard | Assessment Method |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **T1** | Successful deployment of 5 live shops with healthy adoption | Shops must move from sandbox to production and maintain a minimum health score over 30 days |
| **T2** | One working custom workflow in a live business environment | Partner must design, deploy, and operate a custom flow that is verified in production |
| **T3** | One reusable community template with external adoption | Partner must publish a template that is deployed by other partners and maintains a strong rating |
| **T4** | Invitation based on strategic performance | Evaluation includes revenue scale, sub-partner network building, and regional leadership impact |

## 

## Appendix C: Regulatory, Compliance & Data Governance Framework

### C1. DPDP Act (2023) Compliance

Rupantar operates fully aligned with India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act, with **privacy-by-design principles implemented at the system level**.

| Regulatory Principle | Implementation Approach |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Consent Management** | Explicit, purpose-specific consent for each data use case; no blanket approvals |
| **Data Minimisation** | Only operationally necessary data is collected; no speculative or excess data capture |
| **Purpose Limitation** | Data is used strictly for its intended function (e.g., billing data not reused for marketing without consent) |
| **Storage Controls** | Defined retention policies; data retained only while the shop is active |
| **Right to Deletion** | Full data deletion available on request, completed within defined timelines |
| **Data Portability** | Structured export (JSON/CSV) available at any time |
| **Grievance Mechanism** | Dedicated grievance officer with WhatsApp-native access for ease of use |
| **Fiduciary Model** | Rupantar acts strictly as a **data fiduciary (processor), not a data owner** |

### C2. GST and Financial Data Compliance

| Area | Implementation |
| ----- | ----- |
| **GST Integration** | All filings routed through official GSTN APIs |
| **E-Invoicing** | Fully compliant with GST e-invoice standards and IRN generation |
| **Data Retention** | GST records maintained in line with statutory requirements |
| **Auditability** | Complete traceability of all GST-related actions and agent decisions |

### C3. AI Governance Readiness

India is in the process of formalising AI regulation. Rupantar is proactively aligned with expected global and domestic standards.

| Principle | Implementation |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Explainability** | Every agent decision is fully traceable (input → reasoning → output → action) |
| **Human Oversight** | Critical actions (e.g., compliance filings, high-value transactions) require explicit approval |
| **Fairness** | Deterministic outputs for identical inputs; no hidden bias in pricing or recommendations |
| **Accountability** | Full data lineage tracking — source, usage, and model interaction clearly defined |
| **Safety Controls** | Confidence thresholds prevent automated actions under uncertainty |

### C4. Data Localisation

* All Indian shop data is stored and processed within India (AWS / GCP Mumbai regions)  
* No cross-border storage or processing of regulated data  
* LLM inference is routed through India-compliant endpoints

### C5. Data Governance Framework

#### Data Ownership Model

All data belongs to the shopkeeper. Rupantar operates strictly as a data fiduciary, with no claim to ownership. This is enforced at the **architecture level**, not just policy.

#### Customer Data Rights

| Right | Access Mechanism |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Access** | Data summary available on demand via WhatsApp |
| **Correction** | Updates processed within defined SLA |
| **Deletion** | Full deletion within regulatory timelines |
| **Portability** | Export in structured formats (JSON/CSV) |
| **Consent Withdrawal** | Immediate opt-out for any data-sharing use case |

#### Data Sharing Principles

* **Anonymised by default** — no raw or identifiable data is shared externally  
* **Explicit opt-in** — every data-sharing use case requires consent  
* **Transparent** — shops can see what data is shared and why  
* **Value-aligned** — data monetisation includes **economic benefit for the shopkeeper**

#### Consent Architecture (Tiered Model)

| Level | Use Case | Consent Type |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Level 0** | Core operations (billing, inventory, credit) | Implicit (service usage) |
| **Level 1** | Analytics and insights | Explicit opt-in (default enabled) |
| **Level 2** | Aggregated data products (brand intelligence) | Explicit opt-in (default off) |
| **Level 3** | Financial services (credit scoring) | Explicit, per-use consent |

### C6. Regulatory & Industry Engagement

Rupantar plans proactive engagement with key regulatory and industry bodies to stay ahead of policy evolution.

| Institution | Purpose | Timeline |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **IAMAI** | AI governance and digital policy alignment | Phase 1 |
| **FICCI** | SMB and retail sector policy engagement | Phase 1 |
| **MeitY** | AI regulation and startup advisory participation | Phase 2 |
| **RBI Innovation Hub** | Financial services and NBFC ecosystem integration | Phase 2 |
| **NPCI** | UPI ecosystem alignment and payment standards | Phase 1 |
